text: Hello and thanks for watching. Today I'm going to post just a straightforward robotic tap inguinal hernia repair.
Mostly for patient education purposes.
The video shows clips from a previous laparoscopic left inguinal hernia repair, with no visible signs of a recurrent hernia on the left side.
The surgeon attempts to locate the mesh from a previous hernia repair, with a view of the peritoneum where the mesh is faintly visible beneath the tissue.
The camera focuses on the right side, revealing a direct inguinal hernia defect without any signs of an indirect inguinal hernia.
The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, using robotic instruments to carefully manipulate the peritoneum.
An important aspect of minimally invasive inguinal hernia repairs is to reduce the hernia sac itself.
The video shows the hernia sac composed of peritoneum, the thin, glistening lining of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon creates a large flap of the thin peritoneal layer, visibly separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac and its contents, which are contiguous with the previously created peritoneal flap, addressing the direct inguinal hernia defect on the right side.

The camera view is oriented from above the belly button, looking down at the right groin. The inferior epigastric blood vessels are visible at the top of the screen, leading down towards the right groin area.

The surgeon identifies the hernia defect, noting that if it is located on the medial aspect of the inferior epigastric vessels, it is a direct hernia, and if on the lateral aspect, it is an indirect hernia.
This young and very healthy and active male has a direct inguinal hernia on this right side.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the peritoneal flap, revealing the gonadal vessels on the lateral aspect. These vessels are carefully protected as they guide the ongoing dissection.
The gonadal vessels are visible on the right side of the surgical field.
The gonadal vessels are visible, extending from the bottom to the top of the screen, traversing the inguinal canal to supply blood to the right testicle.
A white tubular structure, identified as the vas deferens, is visible on the right side of the screen. It is entering the inguinal canal from a different direction than the gonadal vessels.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents on the right side, visibly manipulating the tissue to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes a small direct hernia defect containing a large amount of incarcerated fat, noting the potential for bladder or bowel involvement in similar cases.
The surgeon observes preperitoneal fat within the hernia sac, noting the absence of bladder tissue.
The surgeon observes the hernia contents, which appear tightly incarcerated and scarred into the defect, indicating a long-standing condition.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to gently tease and release the scarred tissues, allowing the hernia contents to be reduced back into the abdomen.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to separate the remaining scar tissue, successfully reducing the hernia contents back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection behind the pubic bone on the right side, carefully maneuvering instruments in front of the bladder, which is visible below the surgical tools.
The surgeon creates a space in the groin area to lay a prosthetic mesh, intended to cover the hernia defect and other regions susceptible to hernias.
The high resolution three-dimensional image with the robotic instrumentation is just incredible. I was never able to see this kind of detail doing it laparoscopically.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to reduce a small cord lipoma along the spermatic cord, a common excess fat found in males, during the inguinal hernia operation.
On some males it's so large that it can feel like a recurrent hernia or can actually cause symptoms after a hernia repair.
The surgeon finalizes the dissection, ensuring the space is adequately enlarged. The hernia defect is visible, and the mesh is prepared for placement.
The surgeon prepares to place a polypropylene mesh, designed to contour to the groin, over the hernia defect during the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon positions the polypropylene mesh flat against the anatomical structures within the cavity, preparing to fixate the mesh at three locations to prevent shifting.

The robotic arms are used to perform suturing in the surgical field, demonstrating ease and precision compared to laparoscopic techniques, which were previously employed on the opposite side using clips or staples.
I believe and a lot of other surgeons believe that just using sutures like this causes less pain and potentially less chronic pain after anal hernia surgery than using those clips or tacks.
The surgeon places three sutures to fixate the polypropylene mesh in the inguinal region, ensuring it remains in the correct position as it scars down.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap, effectively excluding the polypropylene mesh from the abdominal cavity to prevent contact with the bowel.
I'm going to jump forward here a little bit.
This is a very lightly edited robotic inguinal hernia surgery. I believe this case took about fifty minutes for the entire operation. This gentleman went home the same day.
And I have folks resume all of their activities after two weeks. I hope you enjoyed the operation, and thanks for tuning in.

The surgeon presents the final result of the robotic inguinal hernia surgery, showing the closed peritoneal cavity and the hernia sac. The surgeon expels CO₂ from the pocket to ensure the mesh lays flat.
